metrics.narrative: The business case remains qualitative more than quantified: on the 9 Jun roadmap call the buyer's own procurement lead noted 'Kein klarer Business Case für reinen Sourcing-Einsatz; für InTech Management werden 2% Volumeneinsparung prognostiziert' (no clear business case for pure sourcing use; ~2% volume savings forecast for interface/tech management, 9 Jun 2026). Earlier calls articulate the impact qualitatively -- reduced manual effort, better compliance, and Teams-native adoption (28 Apr 2025, 19 May 2025 calls) -- but no hard ROI/TCO model has been jointly validated. The move to close this gap is a joint ROI/savings workshop tied to the SAP-integration cost debate.

sources: Avoma 24 Jun 2026 AI-based Integration Alignment call
champion.narrative: Engelbert Pölki is an active, vocal champion -- driving pricing negotiation, escalating internally, and per the 24 Jun call 'building internal pressure: daily discussions with boss, engaging controlling, seeking more internal partners to push project.' Karin Eppich co-drove the technical POC and use-case validation. What is still developing: Pölki himself admitted on the 1 Jul onsite 'wir werden auch ein bissl hingehalten' (we are also being held back a bit) by his own leadership, meaning his advocacy has not yet converted into IT or CPO/board sign-off.

sources: Avoma 24 Jun 2026 AI-based Integration Alignment call
competition.narrative: Coupa remains the strongest current threat: priced at ~475K vs Zycus's 448K (near parity), with a history of Post compromising its own process to let Coupa participate (21 Apr 2026 note). The 11 Jun 2026 feedback call found Zycus ahead on Source-to-Contract and AI/agentic capability but rivals slightly ahead on Procure-to-Pay usability. A new threat, Tronkeon, surfaced 24 Jun 2026 as a lower-cost bolt-on intake/orchestration alternative that could let Post's IT team sidestep a full-suite P2P decision while the SAP-integration bottleneck persists. The wedge is Zycus's platform-first AI story (Merlin/ANA) versus bolt-on point solutions.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is well-articulated and consistent from the earliest call: 'Die derzeitige Lösung von Allocation Network wird als unzureichend empfunden, insbesondere wegen des schlechten Supports nach der Übernahme durch QAT' (28 Apr 2025), plus a fragmented, non-integrated system landscape causing manual data transfer and compliance risk (19 May 2025). The pain owner is the procurement operations team (Karin Eppich, Engelbert Pölki), compounded now by an internal IT-capacity pain that is itself blocking the fix.

paper_process.narrative: No MSA, SOW, or legal redline activity is recorded in any call, task, or note. The only paper-process concept discussed is an 'Abruf' (call-off) framework contract fixing prices with staged calls, floated on 25 Jun 2026, plus a proposed 7-year Source-to-Contract term with a 2-year price-fixed optional procurement module discussed on 1 Jul. Visibility would come from getting Post's legal/procurement team to react to a draft contract skeleton, which Zycus committed to send on 16 Jun but has not been confirmed delivered.

economic_buyer.narrative: SF MEDDPICC names Barbara Potisk-Eibensteiner as the economic buyer, and Next_Step + the 25 Jun call show the near-term commercial approver is Mr Flandorfer: 'Post sees tight internal pressures and uncertainty; Engelbert will attempt to secure management time (Herr Flandorfer) to align on pricing/flexibility' (25 Jun 2026). Neither has appeared on a recorded Zycus call, including the 1 Jul onsite where the CPO was expected -- engagement, not identity, is the open risk here.

decision_process.narrative: The process is visible end-to-end in the call sequence: RFI/RFP (Mar 2026 commercial round published 18 Mar), POC and structured use-case testing (May 6-13 2026), feature/feedback debrief (11 Jun), pricing negotiation (16, 25 Jun), and a CPO-invited onsite (1 Jul) confirming shortlist status. The approval chain still requires CIO technical sign-off and CPO/CFO commercial sign-off, neither yet secured.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are extensively documented across calls: Austrian public-procurement (Vergabegesetz) and qualified-e-signature compliance, supplier-portal usability, end-to-end SAP integration quality, AI/agentic capability, TCO, and Teams-native workflow (16 Dec 2025, 29 Jan 2026, 16 Mar 2026 calls). The 11 Jun 2026 feedback session confirmed Zycus judged stronger on Source-to-Contract and AI, with rivals holding a slight edge on Procure-to-Pay usability -- a live, evidence-based scorecard, not a guess.

day_summary.overall: Zycus and Austrian Post met to hash out a restructured commercial model for Merlin Intake — unlimited users but capped transactions — after the deal had already shrunk in scope (amount cut from ~650K to ~449K) and slipped in timeline. Post is cautious: they need a rigorous definition of what counts as a 'transaction' before they can validate whether the proposed cap (e.g. 18,000 in 2027) actually covers their volume, and they want a fresh implementation cost proposal to build an internal business case for their digital roadmap. The ball is now in Zycus's court — Dirk Fischbach and John Woodcock owe Post a transaction definition, a draft pricing file, and a revised implementation offer this week, with a follow-up call already set for tomorrow.

This accommodation is a red flag indicating Coupa has political air cover, possibly at CPO level or above, that could neutralize Zycus's POC superiority and champion advocacy.

16 days to close with vendor selection decision outcome undocumented post-CPO meeting (July 1). No contract negotiation, legal review, or InfoSec clearance in progress. Austria + Turkey cross-border contracting (single contract per Feb 22) adds legal complexity. Required pace for vendor selection confirmation, contract drafting (7-year term, 24-month guarantee per June 29 proposal), legal review, InfoSec clearance, redlining, and signature is unrealistic for July 23 close. High risk of slip to Q3 or Q4 2026.

CIO's SAP/WPS integration effort estimate of 170-300 man-days, raised at July 1 onsite and unresolved as of July 7. Zycus offered AI interface mapping to reduce effort to ~70% of manual work (June 19) but has not delivered the demo or validated the claim. IT alignment call April 22-29 was to 'understand efforts and timelines, no technical discussion', so integration scoping, timeline, and acceptance criteria remain undefined. If Coupa offers a simpler integration path or lower IT effort estimate, this becomes a decision driver that overrides Zycus's POC functional superiority.

Coupa's late entry with Austrian Post process accommodation ('Post was compromising on their process for Coupa to participate', April 21) signals high-level executive sponsorship or incumbent advantage that may override champion preference (Karin Eppich, Pölki) and POC results. This accommodation is a red flag: it suggests Coupa has political air cover at CPO level or above. Champion advocacy (Karin, Pölki) has not neutralized this threat, and the 6-day silence post-CPO meeting suggests the decision may be escalating to a broader executive committee where Coupa's relationships dominate.

AI/Merlin (Anna) event cost structure unclear and raised CFO concern at July 1 onsite ('how we handle invoicing and revenue recognition if they consume more or fewer Anna events than budgeted'). AI/Anna event costs were not in the original pricing, creating cost uncertainty and eroding the pricing advantage vs Coupa. Pölki's need for 'price transparency and security over time' (May 21) is directly threatened by variable AI consumption costs if Coupa offers fixed AI pricing. Requires immediate resolution with revised proposal (fixed costs or consumption caps).
Post is asking for roughly 5x more agentic-AI (ANA) sourcing events at the same flat price, which Zycus said would push margins into the red on AI compute cost.
The CIO's estimate of 170-300 man-days for SAP/WPS integration remains unresolved; SAP resources are consumed by the Turkey and CE HANA migrations through 2027.
Neither the CPO nor the CFO named as economic buyers has appeared on a recorded Zycus call, 16 days from the stated close date.
No MSA, SOW, or legal redline activity has been recorded despite the imminent close date; only a conceptual 'Abruf' framework contract has been discussed.
The buyer itself discussed the real possibility of not starting until early-to-mid 2027 if the SAP resource issue is not resolved this year.
Tronkeon, a lower-cost bolt-on intake/orchestration alternative, could let Post's IT team sidestep a full-suite decision on procure-to-pay.

A lower-cost bolt-on called Tronkeon surfaced on 24 Jun as an alternative that could let the buyer's IT team sidestep a full platform decision on procure-to-pay.

Onsite Teil 1 & Teil 2 with CPO Flandorfer — Dirk Fischbach and Amit Shah (Zycus) met Engelbert Pölki and, in part 2, CPO Flandorfer; discussion centered on Austrian Post's uncertain rollout timeline (driven by a SAP 4HANA migration in Turkey consuming SAP resources), a user-based license model with spend-based analytics pricing, and Zycus's higher-compute 'agentic AI' positioning versus point agents — concluding with implementation estimates of 3-5 months for phase one and ~12 months for full adoption.

Austrian Post's head of group procurement joined the buying team for the first time at the 1 Jul onsite, signalling this has become a group-level decision, not just an IT-team pick, though Zycus has no prior relationship with them.
The buyer wants roughly five times more agentic-AI sourcing events at the same flat price, a gap Zycus said would push margins into the red if granted as asked.

north_star_verdict.math: Today 2026-07-07, close_date 2026-07-23 = 16 days to close. Stage is still Shortlisted even though commercial negotiation (pricing, contract term, agentic-AI event volumes) reached CPO-invited onsite level on 1 Jul 2026 -- the SF stage lags the deal's real state. No Vendor Selected / Negotiation / Contract-In-Progress milestone has been reached, and 16 days is well under the 60-day forecast-critical threshold. No MSA/SOW/legal activity is recorded, and the CIO's own 170-300 man-day SAP-integration estimate remains unresolved as of the 1 Jul onsite (Touchpoint 21/23), meaning the realistic path to signature (IT sign-off -> EB approval -> paper -> signature) cannot complete by 23 Jul on current pace.
evidence: Amit Shah (Zycus) told the buyer 'Congratulations, you're already in the shortlist' on the 1 Jul onsite (Touchpoint 23).
CIO's own estimate of 170-300 man-days for SAP integration remains unresolved as of 1 Jul (Touchpoint 19, 21).
No CPO (Mr Flandorfer) or CFO (Barbara Potisk-Eibensteiner) attendance is recorded on any call despite the Next_Step naming a planned 1 Jul meeting with the CPO.
north_star_verdict.headline: Officially shortlisted and price-competitive against Coupa (448K vs 475K), but the CIO's unresolved SAP-integration objection and an absent economic buyer make the 23 Jul close date not credible.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Pipeline / Best Case at most -- no confirmed economic-buyer engagement, an unresolved IT-integration blocker, and no contract paperwork in motion 16 days from the stated close date.

flags: Champion Karin Eppich is operationally strong but lacks confirmed CPO access. Pölki led July 1 CPO meeting, not Karin. Executive-level advocacy gap.

No quantified business case, ROI target, or compelling event documented. Austrian Post has not stated savings, efficiency gain, or financial justification for 448K investment, leaving deal vulnerable to ROI challenge or delay.

Coupa competitive threat: 475K vs Zycus 448K (6% delta). Process accommodation for Coupa signals high-level sponsorship. Price-driven decision with minimal functional differentiation leaves outcome vulnerable to non-functional factors.

Contract structure proposed (7-year term, 24-month guarantee, Austria+Turkey single contract) but no Austrian Post legal, InfoSec, or procurement contracting activity documented. ÖPAG process and Turkey cross-border complexity add 30-60 day realistic timeline, incompatible with 17-day close.

Coupa received late-entry RFP process accommodation (Apr 21: 'Post was compromising on their process for Coupa to participate'), indicating executive sponsorship or incumbent advantage. Close pricing (475K vs 448K, 6% gap) means non-price factors will decide.

Champion Pölki is fighting the internal IT-capacity battle largely alone; deliver the promised AI-mapping proof point now so he has hard evidence to bring to the CIO rather than carrying the argument himself.

Rising seniority is showing up (a senior group-procurement stakeholder and Melissa Koller joined 01 Jul) — convert that momentum into a confirmed CFO/CPO touchpoint before the next commercial round, since neither has spoken with Zycus directly yet.

Turn the buyer's own praise ('Zycus is AI-leading', 11 Jun) into a written, quantified business case (the 2% tail-spend savings figure) that Pölki can use to win the internal budget fight against competing digital-roadmap projects.

Champion (Karin Eppich) preference for Zycus stated March 16 and reinforced through positive POC feedback June 8, but her influence limited to functional evaluation. Has not driven executive alignment, CIO/CFO engagement, or blocked Coupa. At risk of being overridden by higher-level Coupa sponsorship.

Two new blockers surfaced at July 1 CPO meeting (CIO 170-300 man-day SAP integration concern, Anna event cost-structure question) remain unresolved 6 days later. No follow-up action, demo, or revised proposal documented.

Take the AI-mapping integration demo straight to the relevant stakeholder/IT team rather than relaying it through the champion, to directly counter the 170-300 man-day estimate that is the single biggest blocker to signature.
best_practice_check.summary: Zycus is well-placed technically and commercially but win probability now hinges on converting internal champion advocacy into IT and executive sign-off before the stated close date.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the strongest current threat, sitting at near price parity (475K vs Zycus's 448K) with a track record of Post bending its own process to keep Coupa in the race (21 Apr 2026); Zycus holds an evidenced edge on Source-to-Contract and AI (11 Jun 2026 feedback call). Tronkeon is a newer, lower-cost bolt-on threat (24 Jun 2026) that specifically exploits Post's SAP-integration resource constraint on the procure-to-pay side. The legacy Allocation Network/QAT system is the incumbent being displaced, not a live competitive bid.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Deep, sustained technical and commercial engagement (a completed multi-vendor POC, ongoing pricing negotiation, an onsite with the CPO) sits alongside real structural risk: the Economic Buyer has never engaged directly, the vendor decision is explicitly still open as of 01 Jul 2026, and the buyer's own internal process has no scheduled date compatible with the 23 Jul 2026 close.

deal_risk.text: The 23 Jul 2026 close date (14 days out) is not supported by Post's own stated process, which still requires an undated internal business-case review and roadmap checkpoint.
Coupa remains a live, price-comparable alternative as of 01 Jul 2026.
Post's IT/CIO resourcing constraint (170-300 man-days, no SAP capacity in 2027) is the central unresolved blocker to any 2026 implementation start.

win_position.text: The champion (Engelbert Pölki, with Karin Eppich) has personally carried commercial negotiation for months and is now building the CFO-facing business case, per the 07 Jul 2026 call.
In the 11 Jun 2026 POC feedback session the buyer judged Zycus 'AI-leading among competitors' and stronger on Source-to-Contract functionality.
The buyer explicitly said on 01 Jul 2026 that Zycus is 'already in the shortlist' while still comparing it against 'the others,' meaning no selection has been made.
The Economic Buyer (CFO, per CRM) has never appeared on any of the 22 read calls; access is entirely indirect via the CPO and champion.

deal_momentum.text: A genuine two-way buyer call occurred as recently as 07 Jul 2026 (2 days ago), continuing a pricing negotiation that has run since 18 May 2026.
The CPO and the head of purchasing operations both attended the 01 Jul 2026 onsite directly.
The SAP/IT resourcing blocker (170-300 man-days) has sat unresolved since at least 24 Jun 2026 with no committed date from Post's IT/CIO.
No dated forward milestone exists beyond an informal 'catch up tomorrow or Thursday' from the 07 Jul 2026 call; the digital-roadmap checkpoint itself is undated.

customer_commitment.text: Post's champion is actively engaging controlling and internal leadership to build a business case and secure a digital-roadmap slot, per 07 Jul 2026.
Post is still explicitly benchmarking Zycus against other shortlisted vendors as of 01 Jul 2026, tempering how firm that commitment is.

The opp name explicitly calls out 'Intake' which maps to Merlin Intake AI module ‚Äî AI is in the deal name. Three AI-flavoured SKUs (Merlin, Merlin Intake, AppXtend) is a strong product-level AI signal.
• Engaged BD, Shortlisted stage. Prior run captured 12 Avoma meetings including multi-hour workshops with Karin Eppich, Engelbert Poelki, and EY advisor Christian Buschhueter on the Austrian Post side. LastActivityDate 13 May 2026 confirms active engagement. EY-advised RFP process plus the Engaged BD plus the dual AI products plus opp-name-embedded Intake is a multi-signal AI Hungry profile.
• SF record was too large to fully fetch this run; the Avoma query (date-narrowed) returned an Etex Group meeting under a different oppid so couldn't re-verify the meeting transcripts. Classification leans on Engaged + Shortlisted + dual AI products (Merlin + Merlin Intake) + EY partner advisory + opp-name 'Intake'. Medium confidence pending direct AI-keyword evidence from a verified meeting transcript.

economic_buyer_engagement.evidence: No named economic buyer has appeared on any recorded call within 16 days of the stated close date.

ai_deal_scores_evidence.summary: Zycus is officially on Austrian Post's shortlist against Coupa near price parity, but the deal's fate hinges on defusing the CIO's 170+ man-day SAP-integration objection and getting Post's CPO or CFO to personally sign off before the 23 Jul close date.

deal_risk.text: The competitor Tronkeon surfaced on 24 Jun as an attractive lower-cost bolt-on that could let Post's IT team sidestep a full-suite decision on the procure-to-pay side entirely.
SAP resources are fully consumed by Post's Turkey and CE HANA migrations through 2027; if the IT blocker is not resolved in the next few weeks, the whole deal risks a multi-quarter slip.

win_position.text: Zycus was named ahead on AI/agentic sourcing and Source-to-Contract in the 11 Jun feedback session, and Amit Shah confirmed on 1 Jul that Austrian Post has Zycus 'already in the shortlist' against Coupa's ~475K bid versus Zycus's 448K.

No economic buyer (CPO Mr Flandorfer or CFO Barbara Potisk-Eibensteiner) has appeared on a single recorded call despite being 16 days from the 23 Jul close date, and the CIO's estimate of 170-300 man-days for SAP integration remains unresolved as of the 1 Jul onsite.
Post is pushing for roughly 5x more agentic-AI (ANA) sourcing events at the same flat price, a commercial gap Zycus said would push it into the red on AI compute costs if granted as asked.

deal_momentum.text: A weekly cadence of calls through June culminated in a formal onsite session on 1 Jul, including a first-time appearance by Post's head of group procurement.
Engelbert Pölki said on 1 Jul that his own leadership is 'holding him back a bit,' signalling internal buyer-side friction beyond Zycus's control.

customer_commitment.text: Pölki is actively building internal pressure -- daily discussions with his boss and controlling -- to unblock IT resourcing on Zycus's behalf, per the 24 Jun call.
No MSA, SOW, or legal redline activity has been recorded despite the imminent close date; the only paper-process concept discussed is a framework 'Abruf' contract idea from 25 Jun.
